introduction: A chronic inflammatory skin disease characterized by increased epidermal proliferation and epidermal thickening. Affects people of all ages in all countries. Cause remains largely unknown but has been attributed to genetic, climatic, nutritional, ecological, and immunological factors. Characterized by erythematous lesions with silvery white scales.

features: Eruptive lesions may be pruritic or asymptomatic
Lesions begin as small scaly macules; patterns include guttate, follicular, nummular, geographic, erythrodermic, annular, gyrate, serpinginous
Favoured sites: knees, elbows, scalp, palms, soles, nails
May involve other organs, e.g., MSS in psoriatic arthritis
HIV/AIDS can lead to onset or worsening

drug: Corticosteroid ointments (Hydrocortisone for face and flexures; Betamethasone or clobetasol for scalp, hands, feet)
Systemic antibiotics to eliminate streptococcal pharyngitis
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Coal tar: contraindicated in inflamed, broken or infected skin; causes irritation, photosensitivity
Dithranol: irritant; contraindicated in hypersensitivity, face, acute eruptions
Urea: avoid application to face or broken skin
Steroids: may cause adrenal suppression, tachyphylaxis, rebound effects, atrophy
Salicylic acid: risk of salicylate toxicity
UVL: risk of skin cancer, cataracts, skin aging
Methotrexate: may cause blood disorders, liver damage, GIT issues, renal failure
Cyclosporine: nephrotoxic, risk of malignancies and lymphoproliferative disorders
supportive_measures: Diet: fish oils rich in Q-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids

prevention: Avoid exacerbating factors e.g. abrasions, scratches, harsh fibre bathing sponges, and the drugs listed
Prevent streptococcal sore throat and treat promptly when it occurs
